title: Transpiration — how water moves through plants
examTip: Transpiration questions come in two types: describe the process (know the pull, the xylem, the stomata) and explain how changing a factor affects the rate (always link back to the concentration gradient of water vapour). Both types are very common.
introTips: Transpiration sounds like a dry topic, but it's actually quite elegant — the plant uses physics to pull water upwards without a pump.
Guard cells and stomata are key: you need to explain not just what they do but how their structure lets them do it.
The factors section is very likely to appear in 'describe and explain' questions — learn all four factors and their reasons, not just their effects.
intro: How does a tree get water 20 metres up to its leaves — against gravity, with no heart or pump? The answer is transpiration. Water evaporates from the leaves, and that evaporation creates a pull that drags a continuous column of water all the way up from the roots. It's simple physics, and once you see it, it's hard to forget.

keyPoints.heading: What transpiration is
keyPoints.content: Water is absorbed by root hair cells, travels up through xylem vessels, and eventually evaporates from the leaves through tiny pores called stomata. This evaporation is transpiration. As water leaves the leaf, it pulls the water behind it upward — creating a continuous transpiration stream that moves water from roots all the way to the top of the plant. The driving force is a concentration gradient: the air outside the leaf has a lower water vapour concentration than the air spaces inside, so water diffuses out.
writeBullets: Transpiration = evaporation of water from leaves through stomata
Water travels: root hair cells → xylem → leaf cells → stomata → air
Evaporation creates a 'pull' — the transpiration stream
Driven by a concentration gradient: less water vapour outside leaf than inside
Xylem carries water upwards as the stream pulls it

keyTerms.def: The evaporation of water from the surface of leaves, mainly through stomata
The continuous flow of water from roots to leaves through xylem, driven by evaporation at the leaf surface
A difference in concentration between two areas; substances diffuse from high to low concentration

keyPoints.memTip: Think of a drinking straw — when you sip (evaporation), the liquid below gets pulled upward. That's exactly what transpiration does. The straw is the xylem. Your mouth is the stomata. The drink is the water in the soil. Draw and label this image once and it will stick.

keyPoints.heading: Guard cells and stomata
keyPoints.content: Each stoma (plural: stomata) is a pore in the lower epidermis surrounded by two guard cells. Guard cells control whether the stoma is open or closed. In bright light, guard cells absorb water by osmosis, swell, and because their inner walls are thicker and less elastic than the outer walls, they curve outward — pulling the pore open. This allows CO₂ in for photosynthesis. In darkness or when the plant is short of water, guard cells lose water, become flaccid, and the stoma closes — reducing water loss.
writeBullets: Stomata are pores in the lower epidermis; each controlled by two guard cells
Open in light: guard cells absorb water, swell, curve open — lets CO₂ in
Closed in dark/water shortage: guard cells lose water, become flaccid, pore closes
Guard cell inner wall is thicker — causes the curved opening shape
Stomata opening allows both gas exchange AND water loss

keyTerms.def: A pore in the leaf surface (mainly lower epidermis) through which gas exchange and water vapour loss occur
Pairs of cells flanking each stoma; control its opening and closing by changing shape as they gain or lose water
Limp and lacking turgor — a cell that has lost water and lost its firm shape

keyPoints.memTip: Guard cells work like a banana — when they're full of water they curve outward and open up; when they're deflated they go limp and close. The thick inner wall is the key structural detail: it stops the inner side stretching, so only the outer side expands, bending the cell open. That's the one structural fact AQA expects you to know.

keyPoints.heading: Factors that affect the rate of transpiration
keyPoints.content: Four factors change how fast water evaporates from a leaf. Light intensity: more light → stomata open more → faster transpiration. Temperature: higher temperature → water molecules have more energy → evaporate faster. Air movement (wind): wind blows away water vapour at the leaf surface → steeper concentration gradient → faster diffusion out. Humidity: high humidity means more water vapour already in the air → shallower concentration gradient → slower transpiration. All four factors work by changing the concentration gradient of water vapour between the inside of the leaf and the surrounding air.
writeBullets: Light intensity ↑ → stomata open more → transpiration rate ↑
Temperature ↑ → faster evaporation → transpiration rate ↑
Wind / air movement ↑ → removes water vapour → steeper gradient → rate ↑
Humidity ↑ → more water vapour in air → shallower gradient → rate ↓
All effects are explained by the concentration gradient of water vapour

keyTerms.def: The amount of water vapour in the air; high humidity reduces the concentration gradient and slows transpiration
The difference in concentration that drives net movement of particles from high to low concentration

keyPoints.memTip: Use the phrase 'concentration gradient' in your answer every time — it's the underlying reason for all four factors. LTWH = Light, Temperature, Wind, Humidity. Three of the four make transpiration FASTER when increased; only Humidity makes it SLOWER. Know that exception.

keyPoints.heading: Measuring transpiration — the potometer
keyPoints.content: A potometer is a piece of apparatus used to measure the rate of water uptake by a plant shoot — which is used as an estimate of the transpiration rate. A cut shoot is placed in water-filled tubing. As the shoot transpires, it pulls water up — and this draws an air bubble along a capillary tube. You measure how far the bubble moves in a set time. Important: the potometer measures water uptake, not water loss — but because most water taken up is lost by transpiration, they are very close. To get valid results, control variables such as temperature, light intensity, humidity and air movement.
writeBullets: Potometer measures rate of water uptake (not transpiration directly)
Air bubble moves along capillary tube as plant draws water up
Faster bubble movement = greater water uptake = higher transpiration rate
Control variables: temperature, light, humidity, air movement, leaf area
Assumption: most water taken up is lost by transpiration (close but not exact)

keyTerms.def: Apparatus that measures the rate of water uptake by a plant shoot, used to estimate transpiration rate
A very narrow tube in a potometer; the air bubble moves through it as water is taken up by the plant

keyPoints.memTip: Potometer = 'measures the pot of water going in'. The bubble moves because water is pulled out of the tube by the transpiring shoot. The key exam trap: it measures water UPTAKE, not water LOSS — be precise. If the question asks 'what does a potometer measure?', say 'the rate of water uptake'.

tables.title: Factors affecting transpiration rate
rows: Stomata open wider — more water vapour can escape
Stomata close — less water vapour escapes
Water molecules have more kinetic energy — evaporate faster
Water vapour blown away — steeper concentration gradient maintained
More water vapour in air already — shallower concentration gradient
Less water vapour in air — steeper concentration gradient

1.question: What is the main driving force behind the transpiration stream?
options: The pumping action of the roots
Evaporation of water from leaf cells creating a pull upwards
Osmosis from the soil into root hair cells
1.correctFeedback: Exactly — evaporation at the leaf surface creates a pull that draws water up through the xylem. No pump needed.
1.wrongFeedback: The transpiration stream is driven by evaporation from the leaf. Roots don't pump — they absorb. Xylem cells are dead, so no active transport. Osmosis gets water into the roots, but it's the evaporation at the top that pulls the whole column upward.

3.question: A plant is moved from a humid room into a dry, windy environment. What happens to its transpiration rate and why?
options: Rate decreases — stomata close in wind
Rate increases — lower humidity and more air movement both steepen the concentration gradient
Rate stays the same — the two factors cancel out
Rate increases — higher temperature in dry environments
3.correctFeedback: Right — lower humidity means less water vapour in the air already, and wind removes water vapour from around the leaf. Both effects make the concentration gradient steeper, so water diffuses out faster.
3.wrongFeedback: Both lower humidity and increased air movement steepen the concentration gradient between the inside of the leaf and the outside air. That means water diffuses out faster — transpiration rate increases.

commonMistakes: Saying water is 'pumped' up through xylem — there is no pump. Transpiration creates a pull (tension) as water evaporates from leaves; the cohesion of water molecules means the whole column moves up.
Confusing transpiration with translocation — transpiration is water moving through xylem; translocation is sugars moving through phloem.

Saying high humidity increases transpiration rate — it's the opposite. High humidity means more water vapour already in the air, so the concentration gradient is shallower and water leaves the leaf more slowly.
Describing the potometer as measuring transpiration — it measures water uptake. Most of this water is lost by transpiration, so it's a good estimate, but technically the measurement is uptake.
Forgetting to link factor changes to the concentration gradient — AQA mark schemes usually require you to say why the factor changes the rate, not just that it does.
examTips: Whenever you explain how a factor affects transpiration rate, use the phrase 'concentration gradient'. It tells the examiner you understand the mechanism, not just the effect — and it's almost always worth a mark.

Guard cells: two details AQA often want are (1) they open in light to allow CO₂ for photosynthesis and (2) the inner wall is thicker, which causes the curved opening shape. Mention both in a 'describe guard cell action' question.

For potometer questions, always state what the independent variable is and name at least two control variables. Common control variables: temperature, light intensity, humidity, leaf area, air movement.

videoLinks.searchQuery: AQA GCSE biology transpiration stomata guard cells
videoLinks.label: Transpiration and Stomata

revisionCardBullets: Transpiration = evaporation of water from leaves through stomata
Creates a transpiration stream — pulls water up through xylem from roots
Driven by concentration gradient: less water vapour outside leaf than inside
Guard cells open stomata in light (need CO₂ for photosynthesis); close in dark/water shortage
Guard cell structure: thicker inner wall causes curved opening when turgid
Wind ↑ → transpiration ↑ (removes water vapour, steepens gradient)
Potometer measures water UPTAKE (not loss) — used to estimate transpiration rate
